frameworks.name: Innovation Adoption Gap
frameworks.definition: The gap between technological invention and market adoption is a function of perception and marketing, not product quality; historical hindsight compresses this gap into apparent instantaneity.

claims.text: The future already exists; what we lack is not innovation but the ability to convince people to adopt what already works. When we examine past innovations (mobile phones, multi-channel television, air fryers), we retrospectively see their adoption as obvious and instantaneous, but this is historical compression—adoption was actually gradual and required active market persuasion.
claims.speaker: unknown
claims.mechanism: Historical hindsight bias causes observers to see past innovations as self-evident, compressing decades of gradual adoption into moments of assumed obviousness, obscuring the actual difficulty of moving markets.
bounded_by: Applies to technology and consumer goods adoption
Does not explain how innovations are actually invented, only why adoption appears faster in retrospect

claims.text: We concertina the past when evaluating innovation; we see change as much more instantaneous and self-evident in hindsight than it actually was in real time. Mobile phones were not immediately seen as superior to landlines; each new technology faced adoption resistance that is invisible in historical narrative.
claims.speaker: unknown
conditions: When comparing past vs. present innovation cycles
claims.mechanism: Observers only retain the final adoption state in memory, deleting the intermediate resistance, friction, and time required to convince markets to switch.

practices.text: When introducing any innovation, expect and plan for adoption resistance even when the innovation is objectively superior; view the selling/marketing work as the binding constraint, not product quality.
